title: crepidomanes thysanostomum
intro: Crepidomanes thysanostomum, commonly known as the walking fern, is a type of vascular epiphytic fern native to the tropical regions of Asia. It is known for its unique ability to grow on various surfaces, including rocks and tree trunks. The walking fern has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and skin infections, due to its purported health benefits, which include anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The plant's therapeutic actions are attributed to its bioactive constituents, including flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are believed to have antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial effects.

The leaves of Crepidomanes thysanostomum contain flavonoids, phenolic acids, and saponins, which have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The roots and rhizomes of the plant are rich in alkaloids, including crepidomanin and crepidoside, which have been found to exhibit antimicrobial and antifungal activities. side_effects: Crepidomanes thysanostomum can have side effects if used improperly, such as gastrointestinal issues, including nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea, which may be caused by the high levels of toxic compounds found in the plant's leaves.
